file_name: Mihora and Kalifia - 2025 - Early detection of skin cancer using transfer learning on convolutional neural networks.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) with transfer learning to perform multi-class classification of skin cancer (Basal Cell Carcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, and Malignant Melanoma) using the HAM10000 dermatoscopic image dataset, achieving 96.67% accuracy.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and validation of a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model using transfer learning on the HAM10000 dataset. It reports specific experimental results, including an accuracy of 96.67% and an F1-score of 0.97, and describes the implementation of a web-based diagnostic tool, which are characteristics of a primary research study rather than a review.

title: Early Detection of Skin Cancer Using Transfer Learning on Convolutional Neural Networks
brief_summary: This study developed an automated diagnostic model for the early detection of skin cancer using transfer learning with Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) on the HAM10000 dermatoscopic image dataset. The system was designed to perform multi-class classification of three malignant skin cancer types: Basal Cell Carcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, and Malignant Melanoma. Experimental evaluation demonstrated that the model achieved an accuracy of 96.67% and an F1-score of 0.97, indicating strong classification performance. The research also included the implementation of a web-based application to serve as a computer-aided diagnostic tool for clinicians. The findings suggest that integrating transfer learning within a CNN architecture significantly improves classification efficiency and supports clinical decision-making.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three malignant skin cancer types classified by the CNN model using the HAM10000 dataset.

all_task_types.target: Malignant skin cancer types (Basal Cell Carcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, and Malignant Melanoma)
all_task_types.details: Multi-class classification of dermatoscopic images into three specific malignant skin cancer categories using a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with transfer learning on the HAM10000 dataset. The model outputs discrete diagnostic labels and confidence scores for these classes.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (Human Against Machine)
datasets_used.description: A comprehensive and widely recognized public dataset of high-resolution dermoscopic images sourced from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC). It contains approximately 11,720 images representing a diverse range of pigmented skin lesions, governed by the CC BY-NC 4.0 license.

use.training: Used for training the CNN model with transfer learning. The study experimented with varying dataset sizes per class (50, 70, and 80 images) to evaluate performance.
use.testing: Used for testing the model's classification accuracy and F1-score.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy and F1-score.

HAM10000 (Training Phase - Varying Datas.metric: Accuracy and F1-Score based on number of images per class
HAM10000 (Training Phase - Varying Datas.notes: Training experiments varied the number of datasets per class (50, 70, 80). Accuracy increased with dataset size, but F1-score peaked at 70 images per class and decreased at 80, suggesting data imbalance issues.

HAM10000 (Testing Phase - 20 images per.metric: Precision, Recall, F1-Score, and Support per class; Overall Accuracy and Average F1-Score
HAM10000 (Testing Phase - 20 images per.notes: The model was tested on a dataset comprising 20 images for each skin cancer class. The system achieved an overall accuracy of 96.67% and an average F1-score of 0.97.

architecture_used.main_model: Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with Transfer Learning; specifically ResNet50, EfficientNetB0, and VGG16

architecture_details.feature_extractor: Convolutional layers of the pre-trained network
architecture_details.fine_tuning_strategy: Most initial layers of the pre-trained model were frozen; final layers were retrained

training_details.normalization: Pixel values adjusted to standardized range [0, 1]

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully connected layer with Softmax activation

preprocessing_techniques: Resize: All input images were adjusted to a uniform dimension of 224x224 pixels to ensure uniformity and align with CNN input layer requirements.

Normalization: Pixel values were adjusted to a standardized range of [0, 1] using min-max normalization (X_norm = (X - X_min) / (X_max - X_min)) to stabilize intensity distribution and accelerate model convergence.
Data Augmentation: Applied to minimize overfitting and enhance data diversity, including rotation, flipping, zooming, and translation.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied as part of the data augmentation process to minimize overfitting and enhance data diversity. Represented by function R(θ) in the augmentation equation.

conclusions: Integrating transfer learning within a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architecture significantly improves classification efficiency, even with limited data.

The proposed model achieved an accuracy of 96.67% and an F1-score of 0.97, demonstrating strong capability in identifying multiple malignant skin cancer types (Basal Cell Carcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, and Malignant Melanoma).
The model shows strong potential for real-world implementation as an accurate, efficient, and accessible computer-aided diagnostic tool for early skin cancer detection.
The system can assist dermatologists and clinicians in improving diagnostic precision and reducing examination time in clinical practice.
Data augmentation and balancing play an important role in model generalization, as accuracy increased with the amount of training data.

Class imbalance in the dataset affects the stability of classification results under real conditions, particularly reducing the F1-score for minority lesion types by prioritizing the dominant class during optimization.
The model's performance on image variations from different dermatoscopy devices remains unknown as it has not been tested in real clinical scenarios.
Future implementations should integrate explainable AI (XAI) tools, such as Grad-CAM visualizations, to improve diagnostic interpretability and ensure ethical soundness.

Further research should expand the dataset through cross-institutional collaboration, apply data balancing strategies (e.g., SMOTE or class weighting), and test the system in real clinical settings to assess reliability.
important_remarks: The testing phase used an extremely small sample size (only 20 images per class), which raises significant concerns about the statistical reliability and generalizability of the reported 96.67% accuracy and 0.97 F1-score.

The study explicitly acknowledges class imbalance in the HAM10000 dataset, noting that it prioritizes dominant classes during optimization and reduces the F1-score for minority lesion types, yet no specific balancing strategies (e.g., SMOTE, class weighting) were applied in the final reported results.
The model has not been validated in real clinical scenarios; its performance on image variations from different dermatoscopy devices remains unknown.
The paper lacks explainability features; the authors recommend future integration of Explainable AI (XAI) tools like Grad-CAM to improve diagnostic interpretability and clinical trust.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals were reported to support the significance of the performance metrics.
The training experiments varied dataset sizes per class (50, 70, 80 images) to evaluate performance, indicating a sensitivity to data quantity and potential overfitting risks with limited data.

The study focuses on multi-class classification of three malignant types (BCC, SCC, MM) rather than binary benign/malignant classification, which is a strength but also increases complexity and risk of misclassification among similar malignant types.
